title: Lesson 3.4 — Deploying Your App to Railway

steps.caption: Step 1: Go to railway.com
steps.desc: Railway is the hosting platform where your app will live on the internet. It's free to start — no credit card needed for the free tier.

steps.caption: Step 1: Railway Homepage
steps.desc: This is Railway. It's where thousands of developers deploy their apps. Notice the tagline — 'Deploy apps globally, instantly'. That's exactly what we're doing. Click 'Login' in the top right to get started.

steps.caption: Step 2: Go to Login
steps.desc: Navigate to the Railway login page.

steps.caption: Step 2: Railway Login Page
steps.desc: Railway lets you sign in with GitHub, Google, or email. We strongly recommend GitHub — it connects your code repository to Railway automatically, which is how you deploy later. If you don't have GitHub yet, we'll set that up first.

steps.caption: Step 3: GitHub — Where Your Code Lives
steps.desc: GitHub is where your code is stored before Railway deploys it.

steps.caption: Step 3: GitHub Homepage
steps.desc: GitHub is your code's home. Think of it like Google Drive for your app's files. Every time you save (push) your code to GitHub, Railway can pick it up and redeploy automatically. Sign up at github.com if you haven't — it's free.

steps.caption: Step 4: Create a New GitHub Repository
steps.desc: Navigate to create a new repository on GitHub.

steps.caption: Step 4: Create a New GitHub Repository
steps.desc: This is where you create a new repository (a 'repo') — basically a folder in the cloud for your app's code. Fill in: (1) Repository name — use your app name, no spaces (e.g. my-cake-shop), (2) Set it to Private, (3) Leave everything else as-is. Then click 'Create repository'.

steps.caption: Step 5: Push Your Code to GitHub
steps.desc: After creating the repo, GitHub shows you the commands to push your code.

steps.caption: Step 5: The Push Commands
steps.desc: After creating your repo, GitHub shows you the exact commands to push your code. You'll run these three commands in your terminal from your app folder. Don't worry — I'll walk you through each one.

steps.caption: Step 6: Back to Railway — Create New Project
steps.desc: Navigate back to Railway to start a new project.

steps.caption: Step 6: Railway Dashboard — New Project
steps.desc: Once logged in to Railway, you'll see your dashboard. Click '+ New Project' to start deploying your app. Railway is about to connect to your GitHub and pull your code.

steps.caption: Step 7: Deploy from GitHub Repo
steps.desc: On the new project screen, Railway gives you several options.

steps.caption: Step 7: Choose 'Deploy from GitHub Repo'
steps.desc: Railway gives you several ways to start. Click 'Deploy from GitHub Repo'. Railway will ask permission to access your GitHub account the first time — click Authorize. Then you'll see a list of your repositories.

steps.caption: Step 8: Setting Environment Variables
steps.desc: After connecting your GitHub repo, Railway starts your first deploy. Before it fully works, you need to add environment variables. In your Railway service: click Variables → Add Variable. You need at minimum: SECRET_KEY (paste any long random text — this is your app's security key) and PORT set to 5000. These are private and never visible in your code.

steps.caption: Step 8: Railway Login — Sign In With GitHub
steps.desc: When you first go to railway.com/new, Railway asks you to log in. Click 'Continue with GitHub'. This grants Railway permission to read your repositories so it can deploy your code. You only do this once.

steps.caption: Step 9: A Real Live App on Railway
steps.desc: This is what success looks like.

steps.caption: Step 9: This is What Your Deployed App Looks Like
steps.desc: This is Sweet Spot Custom Cakes — a real app built using this exact process and deployed on Railway. It has a real URL, runs 24/7, and was built using the same method you're learning right now. Your app will look just like this — with your business name, your colors, and your data.

steps.caption: Step 10: The Health Check Endpoint
steps.desc: Every app we build includes a /health endpoint.

steps.caption: Step 10: The /health Endpoint — Your App's Pulse
steps.desc: Every app you build includes a /health endpoint at yourapp.up.railway.app/health. It returns a simple JSON response: status: ok. Railway uses this to confirm your app started correctly. If it returns ok, your deploy succeeded. If it fails, Railway shows you the error in the build log so you can fix it.

steps.caption: Step 11: Another Live App — Contractor Pro AI
steps.desc: Every app in this course is already live on Railway.

steps.caption: Step 11: Contractor Pro AI — Also Live on Railway
steps.desc: Here's another example: Contractor Pro AI — a full job request and quoting app for contractors, built and deployed on Railway using this same process. Notice the URL: contractor-pro-ai-production.up.railway.app. Every app you build in this course gets a URL like this. Real. Live. Shareable.

steps.caption: Step 12: The Update Workflow
steps.desc: Once deployed, updating is effortless.

steps.caption: Step 12: Your Update Workflow From Here
steps.desc: Once your app is live, updating it is simple. Make a change in your code → push it to GitHub → Railway detects the push and redeploys automatically. No manual steps. No server management. No downtime. Railway handles all of it. Your workflow becomes: code it, push it, it's live.
